content:
Case 18:
Initial presentation:
- Age: 34
- Sex: female
- Chief complaint: Follow-up for transfusion-dependent pancytopenia with persistent severe neutropenia and sequelae of disseminated fusariosis
- Vital signs: Temp 37.7 °C, BP 104/66 mmHg, HR 102/min (regular), RR 18/min, SpO₂ 94% on room air.
Setting: Outpatient hematology follow-up in a patient with longstanding PNH/aplastic marrow failure and recent disseminated fungal infection
Capabilities: You are seeing a profoundly cytopenic, heavily transfusion-dependent patient in the outpatient clinic. You can review symptoms, exam, current medications, cytopenia pattern, and recent cross-sectional imaging. You decide on urgency and scope of inpatient versus outpatient diagnostics and whether to proceed to repeat bone marrow evaluation and extended infectious work-up.
Provide either a request for a single information item or solve.
{"response": {"action": "request", "request": "complete blood count with differential"}}

Cbc Chemistry:
CBC: Hb 7.8 g/dL, WBC 0.9 × 10⁹/L, ANC 0.15 × 10⁹/L, Plt 18 × 10⁹/L; MCV 95 fL, MCH 31 pg.
Differential: neutrophils 11%, lymphocytes 64%, monocytes 19%, eosinophils 4%, basophils 2%.
Reticulocytes: 12 × 10⁹/L.
Coagulation: aPTT 35 sec, INR 1.3.
Chemistry: Na 134 mmol/L, K 4.6 mmol/L, creatinine 1.4 mg/dL, BUN 28 mg/dL, AST 41 U/L, ALT 44 U/L, bilirubin 1.6 mg/dL, LDH 328 U/L, uric acid 6.2 mg/dL, CRP 10.8 mg/dL, haptoglobin 21 mg/dL, ferritin 4950 ng/mL.
Immunology: CD4⁺ 380/µL, CD8⁺ 590/µL (CD4/CD8 ratio 0.64). Immunoglobulins: IgG 840 mg/dL (normal), IgA 68 mg/dL, IgM 42 mg/dL.
{"response": {"action": "request", "request": "current medication list and recent treatment history"}}

response.request: current medication list and recent treatment history

content:
History Physical:
2024-09-15: The patient presented for follow-up with increasing transfusion requirements, requiring RBC transfusions weekly and platelet transfusions up to 3–4 times per week. ANC remained critically low at 0.1–0.2 × 10⁹/L. She reported skin lesions that showed gradual healing with postinflammatory hyperpigmentation and impaired vision with persistent blurriness and reduced acuity. She reported intermittent cough with occasional blood-streaked sputum and chronic nasal congestion.
Her general condition was poor with pronounced fatigue, weakness, and emotional distress. She appeared tearful and anxious during consultation, expressing significant fear of renewed long hospital stays, remembering the prolonged summer admission for disseminated fusariosis. ECOG performance status was 1.
Current medication: pegcetacoplan 1080 mg s.c. twice weekly, eltrombopag 150 mg 1-0-0, ciclosporin A 150 mg 1-0-1, voriconazole 200 mg 1-0-1, terbinafine 250 mg 1-0-0, valaciclovir 500 mg 1-0-1, cotrimoxazole 960 mg 1-0-0 (Mo/Wed/Fri), sitagliptin 100 mg 1-0-0, pregabalin 75 mg 1-0-1, sertraline 50 mg 1-0-0, pantoprazole 40 mg 1-0-0, filgrastim 30 Mio IU s.c. 2–3×/week as needed, epoetin alfa 40,000 IU s.c. once weekly, lorazepam 1 mg on demand for anxiety or insomnia.
Vital signs: temperature 37.7 °C, blood pressure 104/66 mmHg, heart rate 102/min (regular), respiratory rate 18/min, oxygen saturation 94% on room air.
{"response": {"action": "request", "request": "recent cross-sectional imaging (CT chest/abdomen/pelvis) for infectious workup"}}

response.request: recent cross-sectional imaging (CT chest/abdomen/pelvis) for infectious workup

content:
Baseline Imaging:
2024-09-18 Abdominal ultrasound: Normal-sized liver (14.8 cm) with homogeneous echotexture, no focal lesions. Intra- and extrahepatic bile ducts not dilated (DHC 5 mm). Gallbladder poorly visualized (postprandial). Spleen 11.2 × 4.6 cm, regular margins, no focal lesions. Kidneys normal in size and morphology (left 10.8 cm, right 11.1 cm) with preserved corticomedullary differentiation. No hydronephrosis, no lymphadenopathy, no ascites.
2024-09-18 Echocardiogram: Sinus rhythm, preserved LV ejection fraction (55%), normal RV function, no relevant valvular disease or pericardial effusion.
2024-09-19 CT of the paranasal sinuses: Mucosal thickening in both maxillary sinuses (right > left) and ethmoidal cells, consistent with chronic fungal sinusitis. No fluid levels, bone erosion, or orbital involvement.
2024-09-20 CT thorax: Multiple bilateral pulmonary nodules (1–1.5 cm), partly cavitary with halo sign, consistent with residual pulmonary fungal lesions. No pleural effusion or mediastinal lymphadenopathy.
{"response": {"action": "request", "request": "bone marrow evaluation results (morphology, flow cytometry, cytogenetics) if available, or request if not done recently"}}

solve.working_diagnosis: Paroxysmal nocturnal hemoglobinuria (PNH) with aplastic marrow failure overlap syndrome, with residual sequelae of recent disseminated fusariosis (pulmonary nodules and chronic sinusitis), complicated by secondary hemophagocytic lymphohistiocytosis (HLH) features (markedly elevated ferritin 4950 ng/mL, cytopenias, elevated CRP) and transfusion-dependent cytopenias
differentials: Myelodysplastic syndrome (MDS) with PNH clone - cannot exclude given aplastic features
Persistent infection-related cytopenia with evolving HLH
Immune-mediated bone marrow failure (pure aplastic anemia overlap)

treatment_plan.action: Urgent bone marrow evaluation with morphology, flow cytometry for PNH clone size, and cytogenetics (including NGS panel) to assess for MDS evolution or aplastic anemia overlap
Continue current PNH-directed therapy: pegcetacoplan 1080 mg SC twice weekly with close hemoglobin and transfusion requirement monitoring
HLH work-up: Ferritin trend, triglycerides, fibrinogen, soluble IL-2 receptor to rule out secondary HLH given ferritin >5000 and clinical context
Continue voriconazole for pulmonary fungal sequelae with therapeutic drug monitoring and liver function assessment
Maintain antifungal prophylaxis with terbinafine and cotrimoxazole as currently prescribed
Continue eltrombopag 150 mg daily and ciclosporin A 150 mg twice daily for aplastic marrow failure component
Continue growth factor support: filgrastim 2-3x/week for neutropenia, epoetin alfa weekly for anemia
Transfusion support: RBC and platelet transfusion support as needed per transfusion thresholds (Hb <8 g/dL symptomatic, platelets <10-20 × 10⁹/L if bleeding/fever)
Ophthalmology follow-up for visual symptoms to assess for intraocular involvement of fusariosis or drug toxicity
Consider hematology referral for allogeneic stem cell transplant evaluation given refractory cytopenias and PNH/aplastic overlap with high transfusion burden

content: {"response": {"action": "request", "request": "bone marrow evaluation results (morphology, flow cytometry, histology, cytogenetics/karyotype) with specific details on PNH clone percentage, cellularity, and any dysplastic changes or cytogenetic abnormalities"}}

response.request: bone marrow evaluation results (morphology, flow cytometry, histology, cytogenetics/karyotype) with specific details on PNH clone percentage, cellularity, and any dysplastic changes or cytogenetic abnormalities

content:
Bone Marrow:
2024-09-22: Bone marrow aspiration showed marked reduction of granulopoiesis and virtually absent megakaryopoiesis. Erythropoiesis was markedly reduced with dysplastic features including nuclear irregularities, megaloblastoid changes, and occasional binucleated forms. Myelopoiesis was strongly suppressed with left shift, hypogranular neutrophils, and pseudo–Pelger-like forms. Plasma cells were slightly increased but morphologically unremarkable. No blast excess (<5%).
Flow cytometry demonstrated a very low cellularity sample with predominance of T-lymphocytes and an inverted CD4/CD8 ratio (0.6). A minimal residual population of myeloid precursors was present without aberrant antigen expression. No evidence of clonal B-lymphocytes, plasma cell dyscrasia, or blast population was detected.
Histology (biopsy) revealed hypocellular marrow spaces (cellularity <10%) largely replaced by adipose tissue. Sparse hematopoietic islands showed residual erythroid precursors with dysplasia, isolated myeloid elements, and almost no megakaryocytes. Small foci of reactive lymphoid infiltrates were present. Iron stain showed increased iron stores and occasional ring sideroblasts (<5%). Reticulin stain showed no marrow fibrosis (MF-0).
{"response": {"action": "request", "request": "cytogenetic results and karyotype analysis including any FISH or molecular testing results"}}

response.request: cytogenetic results and karyotype analysis including any FISH or molecular testing results

content:
Cytogenetics:
Preliminary cytogenetics: 46,XX[2] / 45,XX,-7[18].
{"response": {"action": "request", "request": "soluble IL-2 receptor (sCD25), triglycerides, and fibrinogen levels for HLH evaluation, given markedly elevated ferritin 4950 ng/mL and cytopenias"}}

response.request: soluble IL-2 receptor (sCD25), triglycerides, and fibrinogen levels for HLH evaluation, given markedly elevated ferritin 4950 ng/mL and cytopenias

content:
Extended Diagnostics:
Serology for hepatitis B and C was negative (HBsAg, anti-HBc, and anti-HCV all negative), with protective anti-HBs positivity. HIV-1/2 Ab/Ag screening was negative. CMV and EBV serology showed past infection (IgG positive, IgM negative), and PCR assays for CMV-DNA and EBV-DNA were undetectable.
Microbiology: Blood cultures remained negative. Sputum cultures showed no bacterial or fungal growth. The nasopharyngeal viral PCR panel was negative for influenza A/B, RSV, SARS-CoV-2, adenovirus, metapneumovirus, rhinovirus, and parainfluenza. A wound swab from a healing skin lesion grew Klebsiella pneumoniae (KPC producing) and Enterococcus faecium (VRE), but no fungi.
Flow cytometry from peripheral blood demonstrated a PNH clone: granulocytes 68% FLAER⁻/CD24⁻, monocytes 72% FLAER⁻/CD14⁻, and RBCs 46% CD59-deficient (31% type III, 15% type II populations).
2024-09-20 PET-CT revealed FDG-avid bilateral pulmonary nodules up to 1.6 cm, suspicious of known fungal manifestations. No FDG uptake was detected in skin lesions, and there was no evidence of involvement of abdominal organs, bones, or CNS.
2024-09-21 cMRI: showed no evidence of intracranial fungal infection. No parenchymal lesions or leptomeningeal enhancement.

solve.working_diagnosis: Paroxysmal nocturnal hemoglobinuria (PNH) with aplastic bone marrow failure overlap, complicated by clonal evolution to high-risk myelodysplastic syndrome (MDS) with monosomy 7 (45,XX,-7[18]), presenting with transfusion-dependent severe pancytopenia and residual pulmonary fungal sequelae (disseminated fusariosis)
differentials: Secondary AML transformation from MDS with monosomy 7
Aplastic anemia with PNH clone and MDS-related cytogenetic abnormality
Therapy-related MDS given prior immunosuppression and antifungals

treatment_plan.action: Urgent referral for allogeneic hematopoietic stem cell transplant evaluation - monosomy 7 represents high-risk MDS with significant progression potential to AML
HLA typing of patient and siblings/related donors immediately, while simultaneously initiating unrelated donor search
Continue pegcetacoplan for PNH control pending transplant, with close hemolysis monitoring
Discontinue eltrombopag - consider withdrawing given MDS evolution risk with monosomy 7
Maintain ciclosporin A at current dose for possible ongoing immune-mediated marrow suppression, reassess based on transplant plan
Continue voriconazole therapeutic drug monitoring and maintain antifungal therapy for pulmonary fungal sequelae
Continue growth factor support: filgrastim for neutropenia, epoetin alfa for anemia
Transfusion support: RBC and platelet transfusions per institutional thresholds (Hb <8 g/dL symptomatic, platelets <10-20 × 10⁹/L if stable, higher if bleeding/fever)
Weekly CBC with differential and reticulocyte count to monitor disease trajectory and transfusion requirements
Repeat bone marrow assessment in 4-6 weeks if transplant delayed, to monitor blast percentage and clonal evolution
Ophthalmology follow-up for visual symptoms and potential intraocular fungal involvement assessment

solve.working_diagnosis: Myelodysplastic syndrome with single hit monosomy 7 (45,XX,-7) arising in the setting of underlying paroxysmal nocturnal hemoglobinuria (PNH) and aplastic bone marrow failure overlap syndrome, with residual sequelae of recently treated disseminated fusariosis (pulmonary nodules and probable intraocular involvement), presenting as severe transfusion-dependent pancytopenia with high-risk clonal evolution
differentials: Secondary MDS from prior immunosuppressive therapy with underlying clonal hematopoiesis (TET2/DNMT3A)
Aplastic anemia with MDS-related cytogenetic abnormality (monosomy 7)

treatment_plan.action: Urgent allogeneic hematopoietic stem cell transplant consultation - monosomy 7 in MDS confers high risk of progression to AML and requires immediate transplant evaluation; this is the only curative option
Complete HLA typing (patient, siblings) and initiate unrelated donor search immediately; consider haploidentical related donor as alternative given urgency
Ophthalmology evaluation for intraocular fusariosis - visual symptoms warrant urgent assessment for potential endophthalmitis requiring intravitreal antifungals before transplant conditioning

Continue voriconazole (with TDM) for minimum 4-6 weeks with repeat chest CT to document fungal lesion stability before transplant; consider switching to liposomal amphotericin B if progression despite therapy
Hold eltrombopag - discontinue given MDS evolution with monosomy 7 and theoretical risk of promoting clonal expansion
Continue ciclosporin A at current dose for residual immune-mediated marrow suppression while awaiting transplant, with target trough 150-250 ng/mL
Continue pegcetacoplan 1080 mg SC twice weekly for PNH hemolysis protection during transplant preparation period
Transfusion support: RBC transfusions to maintain Hb >8 g/dL symptomatic; platelets to maintain >20 × 10⁹/L (higher if active bleeding or infection)
Filgrastim 30-48 MIU SC 2-3×/week for neutropenia support and infection prophylaxis; epoetin alfa 40,000 IU weekly for anemia
Psychology support and pre-transplant counseling to address patient's anxiety and fear of hospitalization; involve social work for transplant logistics
Monthly CBC with differential and reticulocyte count to monitor for blast evolution or further cytogenetic deterioration while awaiting transplant
Consider low-intensity transplant conditioning regimen (fludarabine + melphalan ± ATG) given prior fungal infection, to reduce transplant-related mortality while maintaining engraftment efficacy
